subject: Biology
unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: Why does the body require food even when resting, such as during sleep?

options.A: To store excess energy as fat
options.B: To fuel basic biological processes like breathing and circulation

questions.hint: Basal metabolic rate keeps the body functioning even at rest.
questions.explanation: Even at rest, the body requires energy for vital functions such as breathing, circulation, and maintaining body temperature. Option A describes energy storage, not the reason for requiring food at rest.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: Food is the sources of energy that fuel all the biological activities. Running, jumping, moving, growing, reproducing, and all other activities require energy.

explanations_tiered.definition: The body requires energy at rest for basal metabolic functions including breathing, circulation, and temperature maintenance.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Basal metabolic rate (BMR) accounts for 60-75% of daily energy expenditure, supporting essential physiological processes even during sleep.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Your body is like a car engine that idles even when parked; it still needs fuel to keep your heart beating and lungs breathing.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Even at rest, the body expends energy on basal metabolism: cardiac and respiratory work, ion transport, protein synthesis, and thermoregulation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Basal metabolic rate (BMR) is the energy required to maintain homeostasis, including Na+/K+ ATPase activity (~20-40% of BMR), protein turnover, and cellular respiration.
reasoning_steps: Identify that resting still requires energy.
Recall that breathing and circulation continue.
Recognize that food fuels these basic processes.
Select to fuel basic biological processes.
misconceptions: Some think food is only needed during physical activity, but BMR accounts for most daily energy use.
Replacing damaged tissues is only one function; energy for basal processes is the primary need at rest.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Resting body needs energy for BMR (breathing, heartbeat, temperature).

glossary_entry.definition: The energy required to maintain essential physiological functions at rest, such as breathing, circulation, and temperature regulation.

flashcard.front: Why does the body need energy during sleep?
flashcard.flashcard_back: The body requires energy for basal metabolic processes including breathing, circulation, and maintaining body temperature.
flashcard.glossary_definition: The energy required to maintain essential physiological functions at rest, such as breathing, circulation, and temperature regulation.
flashcard.revision_note: Rest needs energy for BMR (heartbeat, breathing).
flashcard.teacher_note: Refer to Activity 5.6: "Do we need energy when resting?"
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Basic biological processes (breathing, circulation) need energy even at rest; storage, repair, and vitamin prevention are not the primary reason for resting energy needs.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: BMR is the energy required at rest for basic body functions like breathing, circulation, and temperature maintenance.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The energy required to maintain essential physiological functions at rest, such as breathing, circulation, and temperature regulation.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain that BMR varies with age, sex, body composition, and thyroid function.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Basic biological processes need energy at rest; storage, repair, and vitamin prevention are not the primary resting needs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: Some people believe that nutrition only refers to eating healthy foods. Which important part of nutrition is missed by this incorrect belief?

options.A: The digestion of food in the stomach
options.B: The conversion of food into energy and nutrients after it is eaten
options.D: The taste and enjoyment of food

questions.hint: Nutrition is a process, not just the act of eating.
questions.explanation: Nutrition is the complete process of taking in food and converting it into energy and other vital nutrients, not just the act of eating. Option B identifies the conversion step that is often overlooked.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: Nutrition is the process of taking in food and converting it into energy and other vital nutrients required for life.

explanations_tiered.definition: Nutrition includes both ingestion and the metabolic conversion of food into energy and nutrients.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The misconception that nutrition is only about eating misses the critical processes of digestion, absorption, and cellular metabolism.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Eating is just the first step; nutrition also includes how your body turns food into fuel and building blocks.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Nutrition encompasses the entire process from ingestion through digestion, absorption, transport, and assimilation of nutrients for energy and growth.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Nutrition integrates ingestion, mechanical/chemical digestion, nutrient absorption, transport, cellular metabolism (glycolysis, Krebs cycle, oxidative phosphorylation), and elimination.
reasoning_steps: Identify the misconception: nutrition = eating healthy.
Recall the full definition of nutrition.
Recognize that conversion into energy is missed.
Select conversion of food into energy and nutrients.
misconceptions: Some think digestion is the missing part, but nutrition includes both digestion and metabolism.
Taste and enjoyment are not part of the scientific definition of nutrition.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Nutrition = eating + converting food into energy + nutrients.

glossary_entry.definition: The process of taking in food and converting it into energy and other vital nutrients required for life.

flashcard.front: What is the full definition of nutrition?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Nutrition is the process of taking in food AND converting it into energy and other vital nutrients required for life.
flashcard.glossary_definition: The process of taking in food and converting it into energy and other vital nutrients required for life.
flashcard.teacher_note: Emphasize that nutrition includes metabolic conversion, not just eating.
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Nutrition includes conversion of food into energy; digestion is part but not the only missed step; waste elimination is part but not the primary overlooked aspect; taste is not part of the definition.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is a common misconception about nutrition?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Some think nutrition only means eating healthy, but it also includes converting food into energy and nutrients.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The process of taking in food and converting it into energy and other vital nutrients required for life.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Address the misconception that nutrition equals diet; it is a physiological process.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Nutrition includes conversion into energy; digestion is part but not the only overlooked aspect; waste elimination is part but not primary; taste is not part of definition.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: A food label lists the following: carbohydrates 30g, protein 10g, fat 15g, vitamin C 60mg, iron 8mg. Which nutrients are classified as micronutrients?
questions.hint: Micronutrients are needed in small amounts.
questions.explanation: Micronutrients are vitamins and minerals required in small quantities; vitamin C and iron are examples. Options A, B, and D list macronutrients (carbohydrates, protein, fat) needed in large amounts.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: The three nutrients, carbohydrate, protein and fats are required in large quantities. Such nutrients are called macronutrients. In contrast, minerals and vitamins are needed in very small quantities and are called micronutrients.

explanations_tiered.definition: Micronutrients are vitamins and minerals required in small quantities, such as vitamin C and iron.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Micronutrients are essential in milligram or microgram amounts, whereas macronutrients are needed in gram quantities daily.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Micronutrients are the tiny helpers your body needs in small amounts, like vitamins and minerals.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Vitamin C and iron are micronutrients because they are required in small quantities (mg or μg), unlike carbohydrates, proteins, and fats which are macronutrients (g).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Micronutrients function as cofactors for enzymes (e.g., iron in catalase) or as signaling molecules (e.g., retinoic acid), required in trace amounts for metabolic regulation.
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify which nutrients are vitamins or minerals.
Step 2: Vitamin C is a vitamin; iron is a mineral.
Step 3: Vitamins and minerals are micronutrients.
reasoning_steps: Identify that micronutrients are vitamins and minerals.
Scan the list for vitamins and minerals.
Vitamin C and iron are micronutrients.
Select vitamin C and iron.
misconceptions: Some think protein can be a micronutrient, but it is a macronutrient.
All nutrients are sometimes thought to be equally needed, but micronutrients are needed in much smaller amounts.

glossary_entry.definition: Vitamins and minerals required in small quantities for normal body function.

flashcard.flashcard_back: Micronutrients are vitamins and minerals needed in small amounts, such as vitamin C and iron.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Vitamins and minerals required in small quantities for normal body function.
flashcard.teacher_note: Contrast the quantities: macronutrients in grams, micronutrients in milligrams or micrograms.
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Vitamin C and iron are micronutrients (vitamin and mineral); carbohydrates, protein, fat are macronutrients.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the difference between macro and micronutrients?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Macronutrients (carbs, protein, fat) are needed in large amounts; micronutrients (vitamins, minerals) are needed in small amounts.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vitamins and minerals required in small quantities for normal body function.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Macros = large (carbs, protein, fat). Micros = small (vitamins, minerals).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use food label examples to identify macro vs micro nutrients.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Micronutrients are vitamins and minerals (vitamin C, iron); carbohydrates, protein, fat are macronutrients.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: A child has a diet that provides enough calories from carbohydrates but very little protein. Which deficiency disease is most likely to develop?
questions.hint: Calories are adequate, but protein is missing.
questions.explanation: Kwashiorkor is caused by inadequate protein intake even when calorie intake is sufficient. Marasmus results from overall calorie deficiency, not isolated protein deficiency.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: Kwashiorkor is a deficiency disease caused by inadequate protein content in human body especially in children.

explanations_tiered.definition: Kwashiorkor is caused by protein deficiency with adequate calorie intake.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Kwashiorkor occurs when children receive sufficient carbohydrates but insufficient protein, leading to edema and other symptoms.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Kwashiorkor happens when a child eats enough food but not enough protein, causing their belly to swell.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In kwashiorkor, dietary protein is inadequate despite sufficient total calories; this leads to low albumin levels and edema.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Kwashiorkor results from insufficient essential amino acids for synthesis of albumin and other plasma proteins, reducing oncotic pressure and causing fluid extravasation into tissues.
reasoning_steps: Identify the diet: enough calories but low protein.
Recall which deficiency is caused by protein deficiency.
Recognize that kwashiorkor is caused by protein deficiency.
Select kwashiorkor.
misconceptions: Marasmus is caused by total calorie deficiency, not isolated protein deficiency.
Scurvy is vitamin C deficiency; rickets is vitamin D deficiency.

glossary_entry.definition: A protein deficiency disease characterized by edema, flaky skin, and hair changes, occurring with adequate calorie intake.

flashcard.front: What is the difference between kwashiorkor and marasmus?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Kwashiorkor is protein deficiency with adequate calories; marasmus is total calorie deficiency.
flashcard.glossary_definition: A protein deficiency disease characterized by edema, flaky skin, and hair changes, occurring with adequate calorie intake.
flashcard.revision_note: Kwashiorkor = protein deficit; marasmus = energy deficit.
flashcard.teacher_note: Emphasize that kwashiorkor can occur even when total food intake seems adequate.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Describe with examples the deficiency diseases
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Kwashiorkor is caused by protein deficiency with adequate calories; marasmus is total calorie deficiency; scurvy is vitamin C; rickets is vitamin D.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What type of malnutrition causes kwashiorkor?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Kwashiorkor is caused by protein deficiency, even when total calorie intake is sufficient.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A protein deficiency disease characterized by edema, flaky skin, and hair changes, occurring with adequate calorie intake.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe with examples the deficiency diseases
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Kwashiorkor is protein deficiency; marasmus is total calorie deficiency; scurvy and rickets are vitamin deficiencies.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: According to the unit, how much energy does one gram of lipid (fat or oil) provide?
questions.hint: Lipids are the most energy-dense macronutrient.
questions.explanation: One gram of lipid provides 37 kJ of energy, which is more than double the energy from carbohydrates (16 kJ) or proteins (17 kJ). Option D (4 kcal) is approximately the energy per gram of carbohydrate, not lipid.

calculation_steps: Recall that one gram of lipid provides 37 kJ of energy.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: One gram of lipid gives 37 kJ of energy.

explanations_tiered.definition: Lipids provide 37 kJ of energy per gram, the highest energy density among macronutrients.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Fats and oils are energy-dense because they contain more carbon-hydrogen bonds that release energy when oxidized.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Fat gives your body more than twice the energy of the same amount of carbs or protein.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Lipids yield 37 kJ/g due to their high proportion of hydrogen atoms, which release energy during beta-oxidation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Complete oxidation of a typical triglyceride (e.g., triolein) yields approximately 37 kJ/g, compared to 16-17 kJ/g for carbohydrates and proteins.
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall the energy value for lipids from the PDF.
Step 2: The PDF states 37 kJ per gram on page 4.
Step 3: Select 37 kJ as the correct answer.
reasoning_steps: Identify that the question asks for energy per gram of lipid.
Recall the specific value from the unit.
Recognize that 37 kJ is correct.
Select 37 kJ.
misconceptions: Some confuse lipid energy density (37 kJ/g) with carbohydrates (16 kJ/g).
17 kJ/g is the value for protein, not lipid.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Lipids = 37 kJ/g (most energy-dense). Carbs = 16 kJ/g. Protein = 17 kJ/g.

glossary_entry.definition: Lipids provide 37 kJ of energy per gram, the highest of the macronutrients.

flashcard.front: How much energy does 1 gram of fat provide?
flashcard.flashcard_back: One gram of fat (lipid) provides 37 kJ of energy, more than double that of carbohydrates or protein.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Lipids provide 37 kJ of energy per gram, the highest of the macronutrients.
flashcard.teacher_note: Compare energy densities: fat 37 kJ/g, carbs 16 kJ/g, protein 17 kJ/g.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Describe the energy provided by nutrients
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Lipids provide 37 kJ/g; carbohydrates 16 kJ/g; proteins 17 kJ/g; 4 kcal is approximately 17 kJ.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which macronutrient provides the most energy per gram?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Lipids (fats and oils) provide 37 kJ per gram, the highest energy density.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Lipids provide 37 kJ of energy per gram, the highest of the macronutrients.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain why fats are important for energy storage and insulation.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the energy provided by nutrients
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Lipids provide 37 kJ/g; carbohydrates 16 kJ/g; proteins 17 kJ/g.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.subsection: Diseases caused by deficiency of minerals and vitamins
questions.question: A child has bowed legs and soft bones. A blood test shows normal calcium levels. Which vitamin deficiency most likely explains the bone deformities?
questions.hint: This vitamin is needed for calcium absorption even if calcium is present in the blood.
questions.explanation: Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets because vitamin D is required for calcium absorption from the gut; without it, calcium cannot be deposited in bones even if blood calcium is normal. Option B, vitamin C deficiency, causes scurvy, not bone deformities.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Diseases caused by deficiency of minerals and vitamins
source_trace.content_anchor: Deficiency disease caused by the shortage of vitamin D is called Rickets. It results in deformed bones in the legs of children. Vitamin D help in the absorption of calcium and phosphorus through the gut wall.

explanations_tiered.definition: Rickets is caused by vitamin D deficiency, which impairs calcium absorption and leads to soft, deformed bones.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vitamin D (calcitriol) increases intestinal calcium absorption; deficiency causes inadequate bone mineralization even when dietary calcium is normal.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Vitamin D is like a key that unlocks calcium from food; without it, calcium can't get into bones, making them soft and bendy.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Vitamin D deficiency reduces intestinal calcium absorption, causing hypocalcemia despite normal blood calcium levels initially, leading to osteomalacia and rickets.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vitamin D (1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol) binds to VDR in enterocytes, upregulating TRPV6 and calbindin for calcium absorption; deficiency causes secondary hyperparathyroidism and bone demineralization.
reasoning_steps: Identify the symptoms: bowed legs, soft bones.
Recall that rickets is caused by vitamin D deficiency.
Recognize that vitamin D is needed for calcium absorption.
Select vitamin D.
misconceptions: Some think rickets is due to calcium deficiency, but it can occur with normal calcium intake if vitamin D is deficient.
Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy (bleeding gums), not bone deformities.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Rickets = vitamin D deficiency → poor Ca absorption → soft bones.

glossary_entry.definition: A vitamin D deficiency disease resulting in soft, deformed bones due to impaired calcium absorption.

flashcard.front: Why does vitamin D deficiency cause rickets?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Vitamin D is needed for calcium absorption; without it, bones cannot mineralize properly, leading to soft, bowed bones.
flashcard.glossary_definition: A vitamin D deficiency disease resulting in soft, deformed bones due to impaired calcium absorption.
flashcard.revision_note: Vitamin D deficiency → poor calcium absorption → rickets.
flashcard.teacher_note: Explain that sunlight exposure helps the body produce vitamin D.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Describe with examples the deficiency diseases of vitamins and minerals
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets; vitamin A affects vision; vitamin C causes scurvy; B12 causes pernicious anemia.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the function of vitamin D?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Vitamin D helps absorb calcium and phosphorus from the gut, which is essential for building strong bones and teeth.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A vitamin D deficiency disease resulting in soft, deformed bones due to impaired calcium absorption.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Vitamin D → calcium absorption → strong bones.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss dietary sources: fish oil, butter, milk, eggs, and sunlight.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe with examples the deficiency diseases of vitamins
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets; calcium may be normal but cannot be absorbed without vitamin D.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: Malaria is transmitted by the bite of an infected mosquito. What is the correct classification of this transmission route?
questions.hint: A mosquito is a living organism that carries the pathogen.
questions.explanation: Vector-borne transmission occurs when a living organism (vector) such as a mosquito carries the pathogen from one host to another. Option A, direct contact, requires person-to-person touch, not an intermediate organism.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: Malaria is caused by protozoa known as plasmodium Protozoa... Female Anopheles mosquitoes feed on human blood... injects the plasmodium with her saliva.

explanations_tiered.definition: Malaria is transmitted via vector-borne transmission, where an infected mosquito transmits the pathogen.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A vector is an organism (e.g., mosquito) that carries a disease-causing agent from an infected host to a susceptible host.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The mosquito acts like a flying syringe, picking up the malaria parasite from one person and injecting it into another.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Vector-borne transmission involves an arthropod vector (Anopheles mosquito) that transmits Plasmodium during a blood meal.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Anopheles mosquito is a biological vector in which Plasmodium undergoes sexual reproduction, then migrates to salivary glands for transmission during subsequent blood meals.
reasoning_steps: Identify that a mosquito is involved.
Recall that a mosquito is a living organism that carries the pathogen.
Recognize this as vector-borne transmission.
Select vector-borne.
misconceptions: Direct contact requires person-to-person touch, not an insect.
Airborne droplets are for diseases like TB and COVID-19.

glossary_entry.definition: Transmission of an infectious disease by a living organism (vector) such as a mosquito, tick, or flea.

flashcard.flashcard_back: Transmission of a disease by a living organism (vector) such as a mosquito carrying malaria or a tick carrying Lyme disease.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Transmission of an infectious disease by a living organism (vector) such as a mosquito, tick, or flea.
flashcard.teacher_note: List other vector-borne diseases: dengue, yellow fever, Lyme disease, plague.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission of infectious diseases
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Malaria is vector-borne (mosquito); direct contact is person-to-person; contaminated water is for cholera; airborne droplets for TB/COVID-19.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A vector is an organism that carries a disease-causing agent and transmits it from one host to another, such as a mosquito for malaria.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Transmission of an infectious disease by a living organism (vector) such as a mosquito, tick, or flea.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Differentiate biological vector (parasite develops inside) from mechanical vector.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission of infectious diseases
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Vector-borne transmission involves a living organism (mosquito); direct contact is person-to-person; contaminated water is for cholera; airborne droplets for respiratory diseases.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: Some people believe that drinking alcohol improves their performance because it makes them feel more confident. What dangerous effect of alcohol is overlooked by this belief?

options.B: Alcohol slows reaction times and impairs judgment

questions.hint: Feeling confident does not mean actual ability improves.
questions.explanation: Alcohol is a depressant that slows nerve impulse transmission, increasing reaction time and impairing judgment despite feelings of confidence. Option A and D are false; alcohol does not improve strength or coordination.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: Alcohol is a depressant. It affects the brain by slowing down the transmission of nerve impulses. Consuming larger quantities leads to slower reaction times, loss of self-control. loss of judgment and control of movements

explanations_tiered.definition: Alcohol impairs reaction time and judgment despite causing subjective feelings of confidence.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Alcohol depresses CNS function, slowing nerve transmission and increasing reaction time, which is dangerous for driving and decision-making.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Feeling brave after drinking doesn't mean you can drive safely; alcohol actually makes your brain slower and your decisions worse.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Alcohol-induced disinhibition creates false confidence, but psychomotor tests show significantly increased reaction time and impaired executive function.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Alcohol enhances GABAergic inhibition and reduces glutamatergic excitation, impairing prefrontal cortex function (judgment) and motor cortex output (reaction time).
reasoning_steps: Identify the dangerous overlooked effect.
Recall that alcohol slows nerve impulses.
Recognize that reaction time and judgment are impaired.
Select slows reaction times and impairs judgment.
misconceptions: Alcohol does not improve strength or coordination; it impairs them.
Memory is also impaired, not improved, by alcohol.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Alcohol → false confidence + slower reactions + poor judgment.

glossary_entry.definition: Alcohol slows nerve impulse transmission, increasing reaction time and impairing judgment and coordination.

flashcard.front: Why is drinking alcohol dangerous for driving?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Alcohol slows reaction times and impairs judgment, even though it may create false confidence.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Alcohol slows nerve impulse transmission, increasing reaction time and impairing judgment and coordination.
flashcard.teacher_note: Discuss legal blood alcohol limits and driving risks.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Examine the effects of alcohol use on health and social wellbeing
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Alcohol slows reaction time and impairs judgment; it does not improve strength, memory, or coordination.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No, alcohol slows reaction time and impairs judgment, making driving more dangerous despite any false confidence.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Alcohol slows nerve impulse transmission, increasing reaction time and impairing judgment and coordination.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that alcohol is a depressant, not a stimulant.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Examine the effects of alcohol use on health and social wellbeing
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Alcohol slows reaction time and impairs judgment; it does not improve strength, memory, or coordination.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: A child in a refugee camp has recently lost a severe amount of weight and now has low weight-for-height. Which type of malnutrition does this describe?
questions.hint: Low weight-for-height indicates recent, severe weight loss.
questions.explanation: Wasting is defined as low weight-for-height, indicating recent and severe weight loss, often from acute food shortage or illness. Stunting is low height-for-age (chronic); underweight is low weight-for-age (composite).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: Wasting (low weight-for-height) is defined as low weight-for-height. It often indicates recent and severe weight loss.

explanations_tiered.definition: Wasting is low weight-for-height, indicating recent severe weight loss due to acute malnutrition.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Wasting reflects acute energy deficit, often from food shortage or illness, and is a strong predictor of mortality in children.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Wasting means a child has become very thin in a short time, like they lost weight quickly because there wasn't enough food.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Wasting (low weight-for-height) indicates acute malnutrition from recent inadequate energy intake or disease, leading to rapid loss of fat and muscle.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Wasting is quantified as weight-for-height Z-score < -2; causes include acute food insecurity, diarrhea, or pneumonia, leading to catabolism of body tissues.
reasoning_steps: Identify the key phrase: recent severe weight loss, low weight-for-height.
Recall that wasting is defined as low weight-for-height.
Recognize that wasting indicates acute malnutrition.
Select wasting.
misconceptions: Stunting is chronic (low height-for-age), not recent weight loss.
Underweight is low weight-for-age (composite of both).

glossary_entry.definition: Low weight-for-height indicating recent and severe weight loss due to acute malnutrition.

flashcard.front: What is the difference between wasting and stunting?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Wasting is low weight-for-height (acute malnutrition); stunting is low height-for-age (chronic malnutrition).
flashcard.glossary_definition: Low weight-for-height indicating recent and severe weight loss due to acute malnutrition.
flashcard.revision_note: Wasting = acute (low weight/height). Stunting = chronic (low height/age).
flashcard.teacher_note: Use the Key Terms definitions to differentiate.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Discuss how stunting, wasting, underweight and micronutrient deficiency
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Wasting is low weight-for-height (recent weight loss); stunting is low height-for-age (chronic); underweight is low weight-for-age; obesity is excess weight.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Wasting indicates recent and severe weight loss from acute malnutrition (low weight-for-height).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Low weight-for-height indicating recent and severe weight loss due to acute malnutrition.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss the relationship between wasting and increased mortality risk.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss how stunting, wasting, underweight and micronutrient deficiency
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Wasting = low weight-for-height (recent weight loss); stunting = low height-for-age (chronic); underweight = low weight-for-age; obesity = excess weight.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: A person has type 1 diabetes, which is an autoimmune condition, not caused by a pathogen. Another person has HIV. Which classification correctly separates these two diseases?

options.A: HIV is infectious; diabetes is non-infectious
options.D: Diabetes is infectious; HIV is non-infectious

questions.hint: HIV is caused by a virus and can be transmitted.
questions.explanation: HIV is an infectious disease caused by a virus that can be transmitted between people. Type 1 diabetes is a non-infectious autoimmune disease not caused by a pathogen and not transmissible.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: Non- infectious diseases... are not caused by disease causing organisms. Some of the non- infectious diseases that you have learnt previously in this chapter are: diseases caused by malnutrition like Kwashiorkor... sickle cell anemia, cancer, allergies, kwashiorkor, marasmus, diabetes, podoconiosis

explanations_tiered.definition: HIV is infectious (caused by a transmissible virus); type 1 diabetes is non-infectious (autoimmune, not transmissible).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Infectious diseases are caused by pathogens and can spread; non-infectious diseases arise from genetics, autoimmunity, lifestyle, or environment.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You can catch HIV from someone because it's caused by a germ, but you cannot catch diabetes from another person.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: HIV is caused by a retrovirus and is transmitted via body fluids; type 1 diabetes results from autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells and is not contagious.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Infectious diseases have a known etiologic agent (HIV is a retrovirus); non-infectious diseases have non-transmissible etiologies including genetic predisposition (HLA-DR3/DR4 in type 1 diabetes).
reasoning_steps: Identify the cause of HIV: virus (infectious).
Identify the cause of type 1 diabetes: autoimmune (non-infectious).
Select HIV is infectious; diabetes is non-infectious.
misconceptions: Some think all diseases are contagious; diabetes is not.
HIV is infectious; calling it non-infectious is incorrect.

glossary_entry.definition: Diseases not caused by pathogens, including genetic, autoimmune, nutritional, and lifestyle-related conditions.

flashcard.flashcard_back: No, diabetes is a non-infectious disease caused by genetics or lifestyle, not by a transmissible pathogen.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Diseases not caused by pathogens, including genetic, autoimmune, nutritional, and lifestyle-related conditions.
flashcard.teacher_note: Contrast infectious (HIV, TB, malaria) vs non-infectious (diabetes, cancer, kwashiorkor).
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer A. HIV is caused by a virus (infectious); type 1 diabetes is autoimmune (non-infectious); they are not both infectious or both non-infectious.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Give one example each of an infectious and a non-infectious disease.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Infectious: HIV, TB, malaria, COVID-19. Non-infectious: diabetes, kwashiorkor, cancer, rickets.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Diseases not caused by pathogens, including genetic, autoimmune, nutritional, and lifestyle-related conditions.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ask students to list additional examples of each.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. HIV is infectious (caused by virus); diabetes is non-infectious (autoimmune, not transmissible).
